meta.title: Node.js
meta.description: Node.js backend: server routing, async patterns, error handling, and security

items.text: Are routes organized logically (by resource, not by HTTP method)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Routes should be grouped by resource (users, orders, products), not by HTTP method. Each resource file handles all methods for that resource.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Resource-based organization makes it easy to find all endpoints for a given entity. Method-based grouping scatters related endpoints across files.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the route file structure. Are routes grouped by resource? Is it easy to find all user-related endpoints?
baseContent.exampleComment: The GET and POST for users are in different files. Grouping them in a single `users.routes.ts` would make the API surface easier to navigate.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One route file per resource. All CRUD operations for users live in the users route.

items.text: Are route handlers thin (delegate to services/controllers)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Route handlers should extract request data, call a service, and return the response. Business logic belongs in service layers, not in handlers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Fat route handlers are untestable without HTTP mocking. Service functions can be tested with plain function calls. Separation also enables reuse across routes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check route handler length. Is business logic mixed with request/response handling? Could the logic be called from a non-HTTP context?
baseContent.exampleComment: This route handler has 80 lines of business logic. Extracting to a `UserService.createUser()` method would make it testable without Express mocking.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Route handlers are the glue between HTTP and business logic. Keep them thin.

items.text: Are middleware functions ordered correctly (auth before business logic)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Middleware runs in order. Auth and rate limiting should come before business logic. Logging should come early. Error handling should come last.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrong middleware order causes security holes (business logic before auth), missed logs (logger after routes), or swallowed errors (error handler before routes).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the middleware registration order. Does auth middleware run before protected routes? Does the error handler come after all routes?
baseContent.exampleComment: The rate limiter is registered after the API routes, so it never runs. Move it before the route registrations.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
app.use('/api', apiRoutes); // routes first
app.use(authMiddleware); // auth never runs!

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Middleware order matters. Auth before routes, error handler after routes.

items.text: Are request bodies validated and parsed with a schema validator (Zod, Joi, Yup)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Never trust incoming request data. Validate and parse it with a schema validation library (Zod, Joi, Yup) before using it.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unvalidated input is the root cause of injection attacks, crashes from unexpected types, and data corruption. Schema validators provide both validation and type narrowing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check route handlers for schema validation on req.body, req.params, and req.query. Is the validated data used (not the raw req.body)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint uses `req.body.email` directly without validation. Add Zod schema validation to ensure the email is valid and the input matches expectations.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate everything at the boundary. req.body is user input — treat it accordingly.

items.text: Are HTTP status codes used correctly (201 for creation, 204 for no content, 404 for not found)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use the correct HTTP status code: 200 for success, 201 for creation, 204 for no content, 400 for bad input, 404 for not found, 500 for server errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Correct status codes make APIs predictable and debuggable. Clients and monitoring tools rely on them. Returning 200 for everything hides errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check response status codes. Does a POST that creates a resource return 201? Does a DELETE return 204? Are error codes specific (not all 500)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This create endpoint returns 200 instead of 201. Using `res.status(201).json(user)` follows REST conventions and helps clients distinguish creates from updates.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: HTTP status codes are part of your API's contract. Use them correctly.

items.text: Are consistent error response shapes used across all endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All error responses should follow the same JSON structure so clients can handle errors uniformly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent error shapes force clients to handle every endpoint's errors differently. A standard shape simplifies client-side error handling and monitoring.
baseContent.howToVerify: Compare error responses across endpoints. Do they all return the same shape (e.g., `{ error: { code, message, details } }`)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint returns `{ msg: 'Not found' }` but others return `{ error: { message: '...' } }`. Let's standardize on one shape.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: One error shape for all endpoints. Clients should never guess the format.

items.text: Are all async operations properly awaited?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every async function call must be awaited (or its promise handled). Calling an async function without await silently drops errors and creates race conditions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing awaits cause silent failures, data inconsistency, and response timing issues. The route may respond before the async work completes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for async function calls without `await`. Check return types — is an unawaited Promise being ignored?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `saveAuditLog()` is async but not awaited. If it fails, the error is silently lost. Add `await` or handle the promise.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every async call needs an await. Missing awaits are silent bugs waiting to happen.

items.text: Are errors in async middleware caught (express-async-errors or try/catch)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Express doesn't catch errors thrown in async route handlers. Without explicit error handling, async errors crash the process or hang the request.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Uncaught async errors in Express result in unhandled promise rejections, which crash Node.js. Requests hang indefinitely without a response.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if async route handlers have try/catch or use a wrapper like express-async-errors. Does the project have a pattern for this?
baseContent.exampleComment: This async handler doesn't have error handling. If `fetchUser` throws, the request hangs. Add try/catch or use express-async-errors.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Wrap async handlers in try/catch or use express-async-errors. Express won't catch your async errors.

items.text: Are database transactions used for multi-step mutations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When multiple database writes must succeed or fail together, wrap them in a transaction. Partial writes corrupt data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without transactions, a failure halfway through a multi-step operation leaves the database in an inconsistent state that's hard to recover from.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for multiple sequential write operations. If one fails, would the database be in an inconsistent state? Should they be in a transaction?
baseContent.exampleComment: This creates an order then updates inventory in separate queries. If the inventory update fails, we have an order with no inventory deduction. Wrap both in a transaction.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
await db.orders.create(order); // succeeds
await db.inventory.decrement(item.id); // fails — order exists without inventory change

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Multi-step writes need transactions. Partial success is often worse than total failure.

items.text: Are long-running operations offloaded to background jobs/queues?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Operations that take more than a few seconds (email sending, PDF generation, image processing) should run in background jobs, not in the request handler.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Long-running operations block the request, consume server resources, and risk timeouts. Background jobs can retry on failure and run independently.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for slow operations in route handlers. Could they be moved to a job queue (Bull, BullMQ, Agenda)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PDF generation takes 5+ seconds and blocks the response. Queue it as a background job and return immediately with a job ID the client can poll.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If it takes more than a second, it probably belongs in a background job.

items.text: Are connection pools used for databases and external services?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Database and HTTP connections should be pooled and reused, not created fresh for every request.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Creating a new connection per request is slow (TCP + TLS handshake) and exhausts connection limits under load. Pools maintain warm connections.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check how database clients and HTTP clients are created. Are they shared across requests? Is there a pool size configured?
baseContent.exampleComment: This creates a new Prisma client instance per request. Use a shared singleton to reuse the connection pool.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Reuse connections. One client instance shared across requests, not one per request.

items.text: Are timeouts set on external HTTP calls?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every outbound HTTP request should have a timeout. Without one, a slow or unresponsive upstream service hangs your server indefinitely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing timeouts cascade — one slow upstream blocks your request, which blocks the client, which may retry, creating more blocked requests. Timeouts prevent cascade failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check outbound HTTP calls (fetch, axios, got). Do they have timeout configuration? What's the default?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `fetch` to the payment provider has no timeout. If the service is slow, our server hangs. Add a timeout of 5-10 seconds.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always set timeouts on external calls. 5 seconds is a reasonable default.

items.text: Is there a global error handler middleware?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Express apps need a global error handler middleware (4 parameters: err, req, res, next) as the last middleware to catch all unhandled errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a global handler, unhandled errors return Express's default HTML error page or crash the process. A proper handler returns structured JSON errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the app has a final error-handling middleware. Does it handle both operational and unexpected errors? Does it log appropriately?
baseContent.exampleComment: There's no global error handler. When an async handler throws, the client gets an HTML stack trace. Add an error middleware as the last `app.use()`.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every Express app needs a global error handler. It's your safety net.

items.text: Are operational errors (expected) distinguished from programmer errors (bugs)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Operational errors (bad user input, network failure) are expected and handled. Programmer errors (null reference, type error) are bugs that should crash and restart.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Handling programmer errors masks bugs. Crashing on operational errors loses availability. Distinguishing them ensures the right response for each.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error handling code. Does it distinguish between expected errors (validation failures, not found) and unexpected errors (uncaught exceptions)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This catch block handles all errors the same way. A validation error (operational) should return 400, while a TypeError (bug) should log and return 500.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Operational errors → handle gracefully. Programmer errors → log, alert, and investigate.

items.text: Are error responses not leaking stack traces or internal details to clients?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error responses to clients should never include stack traces, internal file paths, database query details, or other implementation details.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Stack traces reveal internal architecture, file paths, dependency versions, and sometimes sensitive data. This information helps attackers find vulnerabilities.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error responses. In production, do they return generic messages? Are stack traces logged server-side only?
baseContent.exampleComment: This error response includes `err.stack`. In production, this leaks file paths and internal details to clients. Return a generic message and log the stack server-side.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Log everything server-side. Return nothing sensitive to clients.

items.text: Are errors logged with sufficient context (request ID, user ID, operation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error logs should include enough context to debug: request ID, user ID, operation being performed, and the full error with stack trace.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An error message alone is useless for debugging. Context tells you who was affected, what they were doing, and how to reproduce the issue.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error logging calls. Do they include request context? Can you trace an error back to a specific user action?
baseContent.exampleComment: This error log only has the error message. Adding the request ID, user ID, and operation would make debugging much faster.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Log errors with enough context to debug without reproducing. Request ID, user ID, operation, and input.

items.text: Are unhandled rejections and uncaught exceptions handled at the process level?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Register process-level handlers for uncaught exceptions and unhandled promise rejections to log the error and shut down gracefully.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Since Node.js v15, unhandled rejections crash the process. Even with older versions, unhandled errors leave the process in an undefined state.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `process.on('uncaughtException')` and `process.on('unhandledRejection')` handlers. Do they log and gracefully shut down?
baseContent.exampleComment: The app has no process-level error handlers. An unhandled rejection will crash without logging. Add handlers for uncaughtException and unhandledRejection.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always handle process-level errors. Log, clean up, and exit. Let your process manager restart.

items.text: Are environment variables used for secrets (not hardcoded)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Secrets (API keys, database passwords, JWT secrets) must come from environment variables, not hardcoded in source code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded secrets end up in git history, build artifacts, and error logs. Environment variables keep secrets out of the codebase entirely.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for strings that look like secrets (long random strings, 'password', 'secret', 'key'). Are they loaded from process.env?
baseContent.exampleComment: This JWT secret is hardcoded. Move it to an environment variable: `process.env.JWT_SECRET`. Add it to `.env.example` without the actual value.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const jwtSecret = 'super-secret-key-12345';
const dbUrl = 'postgres://admin:password@db.example.com/prod';

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never hardcode secrets. Environment variables for config, .env files for local dev, secret managers for production.

items.text: Is CORS configured correctly (not `*` in production)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CORS must be configured to allow only your own domains, not `*` (any origin) in production.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` means any website can make authenticated requests to your API, enabling CSRF-like attacks and data theft.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CORS configuration. Is the origin whitelist appropriate? Is `*` only used in development?
baseContent.exampleComment: CORS is set to `origin: '*'`. In production, this allows any website to call your API with the user's cookies. Set specific allowed origins.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: app.use(cors({ origin: '*' })); // any website can call your API

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: CORS origin: '*' is almost never correct in production. Whitelist your domains.

items.text: Are rate limiters in place for public endpoints?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Public-facing endpoints need rate limiting to prevent abuse — brute force attacks, scraping, and denial of service.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without rate limiting, an attacker can send thousands of requests per second to brute-force passwords, scrape data, or overwhelm your server.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if rate limiting middleware is applied to public endpoints (login, signup, password reset, public APIs).
baseContent.exampleComment: The login endpoint has no rate limiting. An attacker could try thousands of passwords per second. Add rate limiting (e.g., 5 attempts per minute per IP).

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Rate limit all public endpoints. Login and password reset are the highest priority.

items.text: Are request body size limits set?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set maximum request body sizes to prevent attackers from sending massive payloads that consume memory and crash the server.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without body size limits, an attacker can send a 10GB JSON payload, exhausting server memory. This is a simple denial-of-service attack.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check Express body parser configuration. Is there a `limit` set? Is it appropriate for the endpoint's needs?
baseContent.exampleComment: No body size limit is configured. The default may be too large. Add `express.json({ limit: '1mb' })` to prevent oversized payloads.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: app.use(express.json()); // default limit may be too large

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set body size limits. 1MB is reasonable for most JSON APIs. Increase only where needed.

items.text: Is input sanitized to prevent NoSQL injection, XSS, and path traversal?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All user input must be sanitized against injection attacks: NoSQL injection (MongoDB), XSS (HTML in output), and path traversal (file operations).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Injection attacks are consistently in OWASP's top 10. They can leak data, modify data, execute code, or access files outside the intended directory.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if user input is used directly in database queries, HTML output, or file paths. Is it sanitized or parameterized?
baseContent.exampleComment: This MongoDB query uses `req.body.email` directly in a `$where` clause. This is vulnerable to NoSQL injection. Use standard query operators instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never trust user input. Validate types, sanitize content, parameterize queries.

items.text: Are authentication tokens validated on every request (JWT expiry, signature)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: JWT tokens must be validated on every request: check signature, expiry, issuer, and audience. Don't just decode — verify.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A decoded but unverified JWT can be forged by anyone. Expired tokens should be rejected. Missing validation is the same as having no auth.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check JWT middleware. Does it verify the signature? Check expiry? Validate issuer/audience claims? What happens with invalid tokens?
baseContent.exampleComment: This middleware uses `jwt.decode()` instead of `jwt.verify()`. Decode doesn't check the signature — anyone can create a valid-looking token. Use `verify()` instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const payload = jwt.decode(token); // no signature verification!
req.user = payload;

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: jwt.verify(), never jwt.decode(). Decode is for debugging, verify is for security.

items.text: Are passwords hashed with bcrypt/scrypt/argon2 (not MD5/SHA)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Passwords must be hashed with a slow, salted algorithm (bcrypt, scrypt, argon2). Never use MD5, SHA-1, or SHA-256 for passwords.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Fast hashes (MD5, SHA) can be brute-forced at billions per second. bcrypt is intentionally slow, making brute-force attacks impractical.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for password handling code. Is bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 used? Check the cost factor (bcrypt rounds should be 10+).
baseContent.exampleComment: Passwords are hashed with SHA-256. This is a fast hash — an attacker can try billions of guesses per second. Switch to bcrypt with at least 10 rounds.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
import crypto from 'crypto';
const hash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(password).digest('hex');

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2. Never MD5, SHA, or plain text. This is non-negotiable.

items.text: Are HTTP security headers set (Helmet.js or equivalent)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Set security headers: X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, and others. Helmet.js sets sensible defaults.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Security headers prevent clickjacking, MIME-type sniffing, and other browser-based attacks with minimal effort.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if Helmet.js or equivalent is used. Are the defaults appropriate? Are any headers explicitly disabled?
baseContent.exampleComment: The app doesn't set security headers. Adding `app.use(helmet())` provides sensible defaults with one line.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import helmet from 'helmet';
app.use(helmet());

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Helmet.js. One line of code, many security headers. There's no reason not to.

items.text: Are file uploads validated (type, size, filename sanitization)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: File uploads must validate MIME type, file size, and filename. Filenames should be sanitized to prevent path traversal attacks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Malicious uploads can execute server-side code (uploading a .php file), traverse directories (`../../etc/passwd`), or fill disk space (no size limit).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check file upload handling. Is the type whitelist enforced (not just the extension)? Is size limited? Is the filename sanitized?
baseContent.exampleComment: This upload handler doesn't validate file type or size. An attacker could upload a 10GB file or a malicious script. Add multer with type and size limits.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const upload = multer({ dest: 'uploads/' }); // no limits
router.post('/upload', upload.single('file'), handler);

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate type, size, and filename on every upload. Trust nothing from the client.

items.text: Are API endpoints tested with supertest or similar?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API endpoints should have integration tests that make real HTTP requests against the server and verify responses.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unit tests for route handlers don't test middleware, serialization, error handling, or the full request lifecycle. Integration tests catch these gaps.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for API endpoint tests. Are they using supertest (or similar) to make real HTTP requests? Do they test success and error cases?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new endpoint doesn't have integration tests. A supertest suite testing the happy path and common error cases would catch middleware and serialization issues.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test endpoints end-to-end with supertest. Unit tests on handlers miss the middleware stack.

items.text: Are critical user flows covered by E2E tests (Playwright, Cypress)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The most important user flows (signup, login, checkout, core feature) should have end-to-end tests that run in a real browser.
baseContent.whyItMatters: E2E tests catch integration issues between frontend and backend that unit and component tests miss — broken deploys, API contract changes, and auth flows.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the critical flow affected by this PR has E2E coverage. Is the new feature tested end-to-end?
baseContent.exampleComment: This changes the checkout flow but there's no E2E test covering it. Adding a Playwright test for the happy path would catch integration issues.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: E2E test your critical paths. Login, core feature, and payment flows are the highest priority.

items.text: Are E2E tests not brittle (no hardcoded waits, stable selectors)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: E2E tests should wait for elements and conditions, not use hardcoded `sleep()` waits. Selectors should be stable (data-testid, roles) not fragile (CSS classes, XPath).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Brittle E2E tests fail intermittently, erode trust, and get ignored. Hardcoded waits slow the suite down and still fail when the system is slower than expected.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `sleep()`, `waitForTimeout()`, or `page.waitForTimeout()` in E2E tests. Check selectors — are they resilient to UI changes?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test uses `await page.waitForTimeout(3000)`. Replace with `await page.waitForSelector('[data-testid="success"]')` for reliable timing.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
await page.waitForTimeout(3000); // arbitrary wait
await page.click('.btn-primary:nth-child(2)'); // fragile selector

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
await page.waitForSelector('[data-testid="submit"]');
await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' }).click();

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Wait for conditions, not time. Use stable selectors. Flaky tests are worse than no tests.

items.text: Are test database fixtures managed properly (seeding, cleanup)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test databases should be seeded with known data before tests and cleaned up after. Tests should not depend on production data or leftover state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without proper fixture management, tests depend on the state left by previous tests, causing order-dependent failures and flaky CI.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test setup. Is the database seeded with known fixtures? Is it cleaned up after? Are tests isolated from each other?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test depends on a user existing in the database from a previous test. Add proper fixture seeding in beforeEach to make tests independent.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Seed before, clean up after, depend on nothing. Each test starts with a known state.

items.text: Are structured logs used (JSON format, correlation IDs)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Logs should be structured JSON with correlation IDs, not unstructured console.log strings. This enables log aggregation, searching, and alerting.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unstructured logs are unsearchable at scale. With structured logs, you can filter by request ID, user, error type, and create dashboards.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `console.log` in production code. Is a structured logger (pino, winston) used? Do logs include request IDs?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `console.log('error: ' + err.message)`. Using a structured logger with request context would make debugging much easier in production.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: console.log('User ' + userId + ' failed to login: ' + err.message);

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: logger.error({ userId, err, requestId: req.id }, 'Login failed');

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Structured JSON logs with context. console.log is for development, not production.

items.text: Are new features behind feature flags for safe rollout?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New features should be behind feature flags so they can be enabled gradually, tested in production, and disabled instantly if issues arise.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Feature flags decouple deployment from release. You can deploy code without exposing it, roll out to 1% of users, and kill it instantly if needed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Is the new feature behind a flag? Can it be disabled without a deployment? Is there a rollout plan?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new checkout flow goes live for all users immediately. A feature flag would let us roll out gradually and revert instantly if issues arise.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Feature flags for safe rollouts. Deploy code anytime, release features when ready.

items.text: Are health check endpoints updated if dependencies change?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Health check endpoints should verify that all critical dependencies (databases, caches, external services) are reachable.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A health check that returns 200 when the database is down means the load balancer keeps sending traffic to a broken instance.
baseContent.howToVerify: If new dependencies are added (new database, external service), does the health check verify they're reachable?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds Redis as a dependency but the health check doesn't verify Redis connectivity. If Redis goes down, the health check still returns 200.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Health checks should check what matters. If a dependency is critical, verify it's alive.

items.text: Are metrics tracked for new API endpoints (latency, error rate)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New endpoints should have metrics for request latency, error rate, and throughput so you can monitor their health in production.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without metrics, you only discover problems when users complain. Metrics enable proactive monitoring, alerting, and capacity planning.
baseContent.howToVerify: Does the new endpoint emit metrics? Is there a dashboard or alert for its error rate and latency?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new payment endpoint doesn't emit metrics. Adding latency and error rate tracking would let us detect issues before users are affected.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Measure what matters. Latency, error rate, and throughput for every endpoint.

items.text: Are Sentry/error tracking breadcrumbs meaningful?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error tracking breadcrumbs should provide meaningful context about what the user was doing before the error occurred.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An error without context is nearly impossible to reproduce. Breadcrumbs showing 'user clicked checkout → entered payment → error' tell the debugging story.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error tracking integration. Are breadcrumbs added for important user actions? Do they provide useful reproduction context?
baseContent.exampleComment: This complex flow doesn't add Sentry breadcrumbs. When errors occur, we won't know what the user was doing. Add breadcrumbs at key decision points.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Good breadcrumbs turn 'something crashed' into 'the user did X, then Y, then it crashed.'

items.text: Are database migrations reversible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Database migrations should include a down/rollback migration that reverses the change, so you can revert if something goes wrong.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Irreversible migrations mean you can't rollback a deployment. If the new code is broken, the database is already changed and the old code won't work with it.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check migration files. Is there a down/rollback function? Does the rollback actually reverse the changes (not just a no-op)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration adds a column but the down migration is empty. If we need to rollback, the old code will encounter the unexpected column.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every migration needs a working rollback. Test the rollback too, not just the migration.

items.text: Are environment-specific configurations handled correctly (dev/staging/prod)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Configuration should vary by environment (dev/staging/prod) without code changes. Use environment variables, not conditional code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded environment checks (`if (env === 'production')`) are fragile and easy to get wrong. Environment-based configuration is cleaner and safer.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for hardcoded environment checks. Are configurations loaded from environment variables? Does the same code run in all environments?
baseContent.exampleComment: This hardcodes the API URL for production. Use an environment variable so the same build works in staging and production.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Same code, different config. Environment variables for everything that varies by environment.
